justification: All 5 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-5) with correct invariant classifications, plus a bonus BR-6. State machine directionality explicitly modeled (in_transit→pending forbidden, delivered terminal). Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable. Confidence level of 72 falls within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions.

observations.text: Identified 6 rules instead of the minimum 5, including the bonus 'new shipment starts in Pending' rule (BR-6) — shows deep domain thinking
Weight bracket correctly classified as invariant: false with excellent justification ('a VP could adjust the boundaries without corrupting system integrity')
State machine directionality thoroughly covered — both backward transition (in_transit→pending) and terminal state (delivered) explicitly modeled as separate rules
AC-13 proactively covers the Pending→Delivered skip scenario, which was not explicitly required but demonstrates thoroughness
Edge cases include boundary precision handling (10.0000001 kg) and identical address scenario — practical concerns a developer would encounter
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the weight bracket boundary ambiguity
Confidence level appropriately moderate at 72 with clear separation of high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions

Minor: escalation recommends Option A as 'upper-inclusive (10 kg = Standard)' but describes it as 'standard logistics convention is to include the boundary in the lower bracket' — the terminology is slightly confusing though the intent is correct

observations.text: All 5 required rules identified with correct invariant classifications (4 true, 1 false), plus a bonus rule about initial state being Pending
Weight bracket explicitly marked invariant: false with excellent justification ('a VP of pricing could adjust these thresholds without corrupting system integrity')
State machine directionality thoroughly modeled — BR-2 explicitly forbids in_transit -> pending, BR-3 marks delivered as terminal
15 acceptance criteria all in proper Given/When/Then format with concrete preconditions and expected outcomes
Testable assertions are specific and actionable (e.g., 'A 10 kg shipment is classified as Standard. A 10.01 kg shipment is classified as Heavy')
Confidence level of 72 with well-reasoned assumptions, including noting ambiguities around skip transitions and boundary inclusivity
Edge cases section identifies real-world concerns like floating-point precision, concurrent state transitions, and the Pending→Delivered skip ambiguity
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, covering boundary ambiguity, skip transitions, cancellation state, and address validation depth
Bounded context named 'Shipment Management' rather than 'Shipment Lifecycle' — minor naming difference but acceptable as it captures the same concept
Domain entity 'Address' captured implicitly through Pickup Address and Delivery Address terms; WeightBracket captured with Standard/Heavy/Freight definitions

trials.justification: All 5 required rules identified and exceeded (10 rules total). State machine directionality correctly modeled with BR-3 (in_transit->pending forbidden), BR-4 (delivered is terminal), and BR-10 (explicit valid transition enumeration). Weight bracket rules (BR-7, BR-8, BR-9) correctly marked invariant: false with sound justification. All 16 acceptance criteria use proper Given/When/Then format. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable (e.g., 'Creating or updating a shipment with weight <= 0 must be rejected'). Confidence level at 72 with clearly stated assumptions and low-confidence areas. BR-6 (1000 kg cap) marked invariant: false is a reasonable judgment call — the rubric expects weight > 0 and <= 1000 as a single invariant: true rule, but splitting it and marking the upper cap as policy-adjustable is a defensible domain interpretation, not a failure.

observations.text: Exceptional rule coverage: 10 rules extracted vs 5 expected, including the often-missed 'newly created shipment enters Pending' rule and the explicit valid-transition-set rule (BR-10)
State machine modeled with full directionality — BR-3 explicitly forbids in_transit->pending, BR-4 covers terminal delivered state, BR-10 enumerates the only valid transitions
Weight bracket rules (BR-7, BR-8, BR-9) all correctly classified as invariant: false with clear justification that pricing tiers are business policy
16 acceptance criteria all in proper Given/When/Then format, covering happy paths, boundary conditions, and invalid inputs
Confidence at 72 with well-reasoned assumptions, high/low confidence areas, and explicit gaps identified — falls within the 65-90 range
Edge cases section identifies boundary precision issues, skip-state scenarios, and ambiguous spec areas (identical addresses, weight mutability)
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, showing domain reasoning beyond mere extraction

BR-6 marks the 1000 kg upper weight cap as invariant: false, while the rubric treats 'weight > 0 and <= 1000' as a single invariant: true rule. The split is defensible but diverges from expected classification
Ubiquitous language section thoroughly defines all domain terms including individual weight brackets and state values
